channel.title: Heart Health & Training Tips
channel.description: Exercise science tips for heart health and performance

item.title: Weekly Cardio Recommendation
item.description: We recommend around 300 minutes each week of regular cardiovascular-focused exercise.

item.title: Heart Rate Zone 1
item.description: Aerobic, low intensity (50–60% of your max heart rate).

item.title: Heart Rate Zone 2
item.description: Moderate aerobic intensity (60–70% of max heart rate).

item.title: Heart Rate Zone 3
item.description: Moderate to high aerobic intensity (70–80% of max heart rate).

item.title: Heart Rate Zone 4
item.description: High intensity, anaerobic (80–90% of max heart rate).

item.title: Heart Rate Zone 5
item.description: Very high intensity, anaerobic (90–100% of max heart rate).

item.title: Body Fuel Sources
item.description: The body uses three fuels for movement: carbohydrates, fats, and protein.

item.title: Anaerobic Energy System
item.description: Movement lasting 10 seconds to 2 minutes is mostly powered anaerobically (no oxygen required).

item.title: Aerobic Energy System
item.description: After 2 minutes of exercise, the aerobic system becomes dominant.

item.title: Fuel Use During Exercise
item.description: All three fuel sources are used during exercise — the dominant one depends on time and intensity.

item.title: Muscle Fuel Storage
item.description: Some fuel is stored directly in muscle fibers, making it quickly available for movement.

item.title: Weekly HIIT Recommendation
item.description: For heart fitness, complete one true high-intensity interval session per week (Zone 4–5).

item.title: Three Energy Systems
item.description: The body uses: Phosphagen (immediate), Anaerobic Glycolysis, and Aerobic Metabolism.

item.title: Specificity Principle
item.description: Slow exercise trains aerobic systems; intense exercise trains anaerobic systems.

item.title: Why Monitor Heart Rate?
item.description: Tracking heart rate shows which energy system you're using.

item.title: Conversation Test
item.description: If you can talk easily while exercising, intensity is lower. Harder talking = higher intensity.

item.title: Large Muscles & Heart Demand
item.description: Exercises like squats use large muscles and create greater demand on the heart.

item.title: Small Muscle Exercises
item.description: Exercises like bicep curls place less demand on the heart than full-body movements.

item.title: Max Heart Rate Formula
item.description: Estimated max heart rate = 220 minus your age.

item.title: Runners: Lactate Threshold
item.description: Tempo or race-pace training close to race day helps improve lactate threshold.

item.title: Runners: Interval Training
item.description: Use a 1:1 work-to-rest ratio with 3–5 minute intervals.

item.title: Runners: Aerobic Base
item.description: You should be able to hold a conversation during long slow distance training.

item.title: HIIT Meaning
item.description: HIIT stands for High Intensity Interval Training.

item.title: HIIT Work-to-Rest Ratio
item.description: A 1:1 ratio works well — 1 minute hard effort, 1 minute recovery.

item.title: Are You Really Training Hard?
item.description: Monitor heart rate to confirm true high-intensity training.

item.title: What is MAX VO₂?
item.description: MAX VO₂ is the maximum oxygen your body can use during intense exercise.

item.title: The Norwegian Protocol
item.description: 4 minutes hard, 3 minutes easy, repeated 4 times — improves MAX VO₂.

item.title: Improve Heart Health
item.description: Improving MAX VO₂ is a proven way to enhance heart health.
